reasoning:
The most recent yield spread (10-Year minus 3-Month) on March 28 is -0.06. The FRED trend analysis over the past month shows a negative linear trend (slope = -0.0037 per day), with a projected value significantly below -0.06 by early April, yielding an empirical probability of 0.35 for exceeding the threshold in the short term. I use this empirically derived 0.35 as the primary anchor for the initial 1-week horizon. 

While macroeconomic expectations of 3–4 federal funds rate cuts over the next year fundamentally weigh on the 3-month yield—which would traditionally suggest an eventual steepening and normalization of the yield curve (spread becoming positive)—forecast uncertainty increases significantly at longer horizons. In accordance with forecasting best practices and explicit system instruction, uncertainty grows with the timescale, pulling the predicted probability smoothly toward 0.50 for the more distant resolution dates. Therefore, starting from a confident downward short-term trend projection, the probabilities decay monotonically back up to near 50/50 over the 1-year horizon.
